paper_title: Prescriptive Scaling Laws for Data Constrained Training

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Training compute is increasingly outpacing the availability of high-quality data. This shifts the central challenge from optimal compute allocation to extracting maximum value from limited data. The widely adopted Chinchilla scaling law assumes every training token is unique. This limits its ability to guide pretraining decisions in data-constrained regimes. We model the excess loss under repetition with a simple additive overfitting penalty and find that it accurately describes model behavior. Our scaling law yields qualitatively new compute-optimal allocation advice. Beyond a point, further repetition is counterproductive and compute is better spent on model capacity. We show that following our law's recommended configuration improves performance in data-constrained regimes. Finally, because our one-parameter form isolates overfitting in a single coefficient, it enables direct comparison across training configurations. As a case study, we show that strong weight decay ($λ=1.0$) reduces this coefficient by approximately 70%, providing a scaling-law explanation for recent findings that optimal weight decay in data-constrained regimes is an order of magnitude larger than standard practice.

sections.title: Prescriptive Scaling Laws for Data Constrained Training
sections.content: Justin Lovelace, Christian Belardi, Srivatsa Kundurthy, Shriya Sudhakar, Kilian Q. Weinberger Department of Computer Science Cornell University Ithaca, NY

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Training compute is increasingly outpacing the availability of high-quality data. This shifts the central challenge from optimal compute allocation to extracting maximum value from limited data. The widely adopted Chinchilla scaling law assumes every training token is unique. This limits its ability to guide pretraining decisions in data-constrained regimes. We model the excess loss under repetition with simple additive overfitting penalty and find that it accurately describes model behavior. Our scaling law yields qualitatively new compute-optimal allocation advice. Beyond point, further repetition is counterproductive and compute is better spent on model capacity. We show that following our laws recommended configuration improves performance in data-constrained regimes. Finally, because our one-parameter form isolates overfitting in single coefficient, it enables direct comparison across training configurations. As case study, we show that strong weight decay (λ = 1.0) reduces this coefficient by approximately 70%, providing scaling-law explanation for recent findings that optimal weight decay in data-constrained regimes is an order of magnitude larger than standard practice.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Training compute is scaling faster than the supply of high-quality data. While raw text is abundant, the trend across state-of-the-art pipelinesaggressive quality filtering (Penedo et al., 2024), upsampling of curated subsets (Olmo et al., 2025), and mid-training on domainspecific corpora (Allal et al., 2025; Olmo et al., 2025)reflects new reality: data, not compute, is the bottleneck. In specialized domains such as mathematics, code, and lowresource languages the constraint is even stronger. Domain-specific datasets are often orders of magnitude smaller than the compute budget can absorb (Lewkowycz et al., 2022). This shifts the central question from the Chinchilla framing of how to allocate compute optimally in the infinite-data regime (Hoffmann et al., 2022) to how to extract the most from fixed pool of data, treating compute as effectively unbounded. The simplest response, repeating data across multiple epochs, is already widespread. Yet the Chinchilla scaling law assumes every token is unique, and existing extensions (Muennighoff et al., 2023) for data repetition have critical limitation. They can model diminishing returns but cannot represent the regime where loss increases from overfitting. They also do not capture the interaction with overfitting and model size. In practice, larger models overfit faster on repeated data. Without explicitly modeling overfitting, we cannot accurately describe language modeling behavior under repetition. We propose simple additive overfitting penalty that encodes simple intuition: overfitting is worse with limited data and larger models. We therefore model repeated tokens as useful while simultaneously incurring separate, additive overfitting penalty that grows with repetitions. Corresponding author: jl3353@cornell.edu 1 Preprint. Under review. We train over 300 models spanning 15M1B parameters, 50M6B unique tokens, two weight decay strengths, and up to 16 epochs. Our central contribution is an additive overfitting penalty for data-constrained scaling laws that augments the Chinchilla law with simple repetition term. complexity ladder of 1-, 2-, and 4-parameter forms traces Pareto frontier of fit quality versus complexity, with even the one-parameter form substantially outperforming prior data-constrained scaling laws. The fitted law yields qualitatively new compute-optimal allocation advice: beyond data-dependent threshold, further repetition is counterproductive and compute is better spent on model capacity. We validate this prescription by training the configuration each law recommends and show that ours achieves the strongest performance in both perplexity and downstream evaluation. Finally, because our law isolates overfitting in single coefficient, it enables direct comparison across training configurations. As case study, we show that strong weight decay (λ = 1.0) reduces overfitting by approximately 70%, and our law predicts the compute budget at which strong regularization overtakes the standard setting, demonstrating that it effectively guides training decisions in data-constrained regimes.

sections.title: 2 Background and Related Work
sections.content: Neural scaling laws. Training large language model requires two key decisions: how big to make the model and how much data to train it on. Together these determine both how much compute is needed and the performance of the final model. Typically, performance is measured by the models loss on held-out text. natural question is how loss changes as we scale up the model or the dataset. Empirically, the answer is remarkably clean: loss falls predictably as either quantity grows, following smooth mathematical trend that holds across many orders of magnitude. These trends are called neural scaling laws, and their predictability is what makes them useful. Instead of training many large models to find the best configuration,which would be prohibitively expensive, researcher can fit scaling law to many small, cheap training runs. scaling law can then be used to forecast the loss of much larger run before committing the compute to it. Kaplan et al. (2020) first established these trends, and Hoffmann et al. (2022) refined them into the Chinchilla scaling law, which expresses the final loss of trained model as sum of three terms: L(N, D) = + Nα + Dβ , (1) where is the number of model parameters and is the number of training tokens. Each of the three terms has natural interpretation. The first term, E, is floor that the loss cannot drop below no matter how much we scale compute. Natural language has inherent unpredictability (the next word is rarely fully determined by what came before), and captures that uncertainty. The second term, A/Nα, represents the cost of having model that is too small. model with limited parameters cannot represent all the patterns present in language. As grows, this term shrinks toward zero, and the rate at which it shrinks is controlled by the exponent α. The third term, B/Dβ, represents the cost of having trained on too little data. Even very large model has mediocre performance if it has not seen enough data. As grows, this term also shrinks toward zero, with its rate governed by the exponent β. Together, the three terms say that loss is the sum of what is fundamentally unlearnable, what the model was too small to learn, and what the data was too limited to teach. practical consequence of this form is that compute budget determines how to trade off model size against dataset size. Training compute scales as 6ND (see (Kaplan et al., 2020)), so for fixed C, the scaling law picks out specific (N, D) pair that minimizes predicted loss. The exact : ratio depends on the fitted exponents and varies across scaling studies with different datasets and training setups (Li et al., 2025). Crucially, the Chinchilla law assumes that every training token is uniquethe model sees each piece of text exactly once. In practice, high-quality text is often scarce enough that training runs pass over the same data multiple times, violating this assumption. Scaling laws of this form have been widely adopted for guiding training decisions across domains 2 Preprint. Under review. and applications (Ludziejewski et al., 2024; Gulrajani & Hashimoto, 2023; Cherti et al., 2023; Kumar et al., 2025; Aghajanyan et al., 2023; Sardana et al., 2024; Gadre et al., 2025). Scaling under data repetition. The Chinchilla law assumes each training token is seen exactly once, but in practice models often train for multiple epochs over the same data. While performance continues to improve, each additional pass over the data yields progressively diminishing returns. At some point, additional epochs contribute almost nothing, or can even hurt performance due to overfitting. Any scaling law that accounts for repetition must capture this diminishing return. Muennighoff et al. (2023) formalized this intuition by replacing the raw token count in the Chinchilla law with an effective data quantity (cid:98)D. The main idea is that the contribution of repeated tokens decays exponentially. The first repeat is worth almost as much as fresh data, the second repeat somewhat less, and so on, until further repeats add essentially nothing. Let UD denote the number of unique tokens in the dataset and let RD denote the number of additional epochs beyond the first (RD = 0 means the model sees each token once, RD = 1 means twice, and so on). They define: (cid:98)D(UD, RD) = UD (cid:16) 1 + D (cid:16) 1 eRD/R (cid:17)(cid:17) , (2) where is fit constant that controls how quickly repeated data loses its marginal value. The behavior of this expression matches the decay intuition directly. When RD is small, the exponential term is approximately linear, so (cid:98)D(UD, RD) UD (1 + RD)each repeated epoch contributes nearly as much as fresh one. As RD grows, the exponential saturates toward one, so (cid:98)D(UD, RD) approaches an upper limit of UD (1 + D). No matter how many more epochs are added, effective data cannot exceed this ceiling. Substituting (cid:98)D into the Chinchilla form gives: L(N, UD, RD) = + Nα + (cid:98)D(UD, RD)β . (3) However, this formulation treats repetition as purely data-side phenomenon with no dependence on model size. Empirically, this is incomplete: larger models overfit more quickly on repeated data than smaller ones do, so the cost of repetition should depend on as well as D. To capture this, Muennighoff et al. (2023) apply the same saturating form to model parameters. The intuition is that model can be too large for its dataset. If the parameter count far exceeds what the available unique tokens can support, the extra capacity yields diminishing returns. They measure excess capacity relative to Nopt, the Chinchilla compute-optimal model size for UD unique tokens. If the actual model size exceeds Nopt, the ratio RN = (N/UN) 1 measures the degree of overparameterization; otherwise RN = 0. Writing UN = min{Nopt, N}, the effective parameter count is: (cid:98)N(UN, RN) = UN + UN (cid:16) 1 eRN /R (cid:17) , (4) plays the same role as where but for excess parameters rather than repeated tokens. Substituting both (cid:98)D and (cid:98)N into the Chinchilla form yields scaling law with two additional fit constants (R and N): L(UN, RN, UD, RD) = + (cid:98)N(UN, RN)α + (cid:98)D(UD, RD)β . (5) This introduces an interaction between model size and repetition: overparameterized models see their effective capacity (cid:98)N(UN, RN) saturate, which raises the predicted loss under heavy repetition. While this formulation correctly captures the qualitative behavior, the mechanism is indirect. It models overparameterization as diminishing return on effective model size rather than as an explicit overfitting cost, and it is not clear why excess parameters should follow the same exponential saturation form as repeated data. In section 4, we propose an alternative formulation that explicitly separates the contribution of repeated tokens from the overfitting penalty they incur, and show that this decomposition reveals how regularization strength modulates models tolerance to repetition. Preprint. Under review. Figure 1: Existing scaling laws fail to model overfitting. Predicted vs. observed validation loss under the Chinchilla baseline (treating repeated tokens as unique) and the Muennighoff et al. (2023) (cid:98)D(UD, RD), (cid:98)N(UN, RN) formulation across four model sizes at UD = 200M. Both formulations fail to capture the loss increase at high repetition counts, systematically underpredicting loss as repetitions grow.

sections.title: 3 Experimental setup
sections.content: We pretrain decoder-only language models using the Llama 2 architecture and tokenizer (Touvron et al., 2023) across grid of model sizes, unique data budgets, and repetition counts. All models are trained on the FineWeb dataset (Penedo et al., 2024), large-scale filtered web corpus. For our scaling study, we sweep over model sizes ranging from 15M to 1B parameters, unique data budgets UD from 50M to 6B tokens, and repetition counts RD {0, 1, 3, 7, 11, 15}; the full experimental grid is detailed in Appendix C. All configurations are trained at two weight decay strengths: the standard setting λ = 0.1 and strong setting λ = 1.0. All other hyperparameterslearning rate, warmup schedule, batch sizeare held constant across weight decay conditions, isolating the effect of regularization on repetition overfitting. We report the final validation loss at the end of training. Detailed model architectures, hyperparameter configurations, and training procedures are provided in Appendix B. We evaluate our models with validation perplexity and also report downstream performance with the Open Language Model Evaluation System (OLMES) (Gu et al., 2025). We report the average bits-per-byte (BPB) across 19 downstream language understanding tasks from the recommended evaluation suite for small models (Heineman et al., 2025).

sections.title: 4 Scaling laws for repeated data
sections.content: Limitations of the effective-data approach. We begin by examining where the Muennighoff et al. (2023) formulation breaks down. Figure 1 plots predicted vs. observed validation loss under the Chinchilla baseline and the (cid:98)D(UD, RD), (cid:98)N(UN, RN) form across four model sizes. Two patterns emerge: first, the gap between predicted and observed loss grows with the number of repetitions, indicating that the effective-data forms systematically underpredict loss at high epoch counts. Second, the gap increases with model capacity relative to unique data, confirming an interaction between model size and repetition that the exponential saturation in (cid:98)D(UD, RD) and (cid:98)N(UN, RN) cannot capture. An additive overfitting penalty. To identify the right functional form, we fit the Chinchilla law (Equation 1) to single-epoch runs, obtaining parameters (E, A, B, α, β), and use this fit to predict multi-epoch loss by treating repeated tokens as if they were fresh data = UD (1 + RD). The residual between the observed loss and this prediction isolates the additional cost attributable to repetition. When we plot this residual for fixed model size and unique data budget UD, varying only the number of repetitions RD, power-law relationship emerges (Figure 2). shared power-law fit Pi Rδ across all (model, budget) configurations, with δ tied and Pi free per cell, finds the repetition damage is superlinear. Each additional epoch of repetition inflicts more damage than the last. 4 Preprint. Under review. Figure 2: The cost of repeating data grows superlinearly. Residual between observed loss and the Chinchilla prediction (treating all repeated tokens as unique data) as function of repetition count RD, shown for three model sizes and three unique data budgets UD. Dashed lines show power-law fits with shared exponent δ across all configurations; the fitted δ > 1.0 indicates superlinear repetition damage. Slopes are steeper for larger models and smaller unique data budgets. complexity ladder of penalty forms. Examining how the per-cell coefficient Pi varies across configurations reveals its structure: larger models and smaller unique data budgets incur steeper penalties. This motivates family of additive penalty forms of increasing complexity, which we present as Pareto frontier trading off number of free parameters against fit quality. The simplest form uses single free parameter and the dimensionless ratio N/UD: L(N, UD, RD) = + Nα + (UD (1 + RD))β + RD UD . (6) This linear-in-RD form already substantially outperforms the Muennighoff et al. (2023) formulations (Section 5). Adding second free parameter, the exponent κ on the capacity ratio, allows the penalty to scale nonlinearly with model size relative to data: (cid:19)κ L(N, UD, RD) = + + RD . (7) Nα + (UD (1 + RD))β (cid:18) UD Across configurations, κ > 1, indicating that the overfitting penalty grows superlinearly with the ratio of model capacity to unique data. The full four-parameter form adds superlinear exponent δ on the repetition count and decouples the data-budget exponent γ from the model-size exponent κ: L(N, UD, RD) = + Nα + (UD (1 + RD))β + Rδ (cid:33)κ . (cid:32) Uγ (8) At RD = 0 (single epoch), all three forms reduce exactly to the Chinchilla law. The key conceptual difference from the effective-data approach is that repeated tokens play dual role: they continue to reduce the data-sufficiency term (they are not wasted) while simultaneously incurring growing overfitting cost.

sections.title: 5 Scaling law validation
sections.content: We validate the additive penalty laws (Equation 6Equation 8) against the Chinchilla baseline and the Muennighoff et al. (2023) effective-data formulations on two independent scaling sweeps. The first is our own CLM sweep described in section 3. The second is the public scaling sweep from Muennighoff et al. (2023), including all runs up to 64 epochsa more lenient filter than the outlier-removal criteria applied in their original analysis. Figure 3 compares predicted and observed loss across model sizes. Our proposed law accurately tracks the observed loss across all configurations, capturing the degradation under heavy repetition that the effective-data formulations miss. Figure 4 confirms the 5 Preprint. Under review. Figure 3: Our additive overfitting law adapts to model size. Predicted vs. observed validation loss under different scaling laws. Figure 4: Scaling law fit quality. Huber loss (left, lower is better) and R2 (right, higher is better) for each scaling law formulation, evaluated on both our scaling sweep and the Muennighoff et al. (2023) data. quantitative improvement: even our one-parameter additive penalty (Equation 6) substantially outperforms both the (cid:98)D(UD, RD) and (cid:98)D(UD, RD), (cid:98)N(UN, RN) forms, and the four-parameter form (Equation 8) achieves near-perfect fit on our data. The improvement extends to the heldout Muennighoff et al. (2023) data, where model sizes and repetition ranges span wider range. Compute-optimal allocation under repetition. The superlinear repetition penalty yields qualitatively different compute-optimal allocation advice than prior scaling laws (Figure 5). The Chinchilla law, which ignores overfitting, always recommends more repetition: the optimal total token count grows linearly with compute at fixed UD. The Muennighoff et al. (2023) (cid:98)D(UD, RD), (cid:98)N(UN, RN) form moderates this, prescribing diminishing returns from repetition but never recommending that repetition stop. Our four-parameter law, by contrast, predicts compute budget beyond which additional repetition is counterproductive. The allocation frontier turns back: at high compute, the law recommends scaling model size while reducing the number of epochs1, reflecting the overfitting cost of continued repetition. This provides concrete guidance for practitioners: given fixed data budget, there is compute level beyond which training larger model for fewer epochs outperforms training smaller model for more. Prescriptive validation. The R2 comparisons above measure descriptive fithow well each law explains configurations it was trained on. more stringent test is prescriptive accuracy: given fixed unique-data and compute budget, does the law recommend the configuration that actually achieves the lowest loss? For each (token budget, compute budget) pair in Table 1, we solve each law for the optimal model size and epoch count, train the recommended configuration, and evaluate. Our law consistently recommends larger 1On its surface, this recommendation appears to contradict Muennighoff et al. (2023), who find that data-constrained compute should be allocated toward smaller models trained for more epochs. We trace this disagreement to methodological choice in their analysis; see Appendix A. 6 Preprint. Under review. Figure 5: Compute-optimal allocation frontiers. (Left) Observed validation loss across our experimental grid (UD = 100M), with contour lines showing the interpolated loss landscape. (Right) Compute-optimal allocation at = 2 1019 FLOPs and UD = 500M, comparing the Chinchilla law, Muennighoff et al. (2023) (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N, and our law (Equation 8). Table 1: Prescriptive validation. For each (token budget, compute budget) pair, we train the configuration recommended by each scaling law: Chinchilla (Hoffmann et al., 2022), Eff. Param. (Muennighoff et al., 2023), and Ours (Equation 8). OLMES BPB is the average bits-per-byte across 19 downstream tasks in the OLMES evaluation harness (Gu et al., 2025). UD Scaling law Params Epochs Perplexity OLMES BPB 250M 5 1018 500M 1 1019 2 1019 Chinchilla Eff. Param. Ours Chinchilla Eff. Param. Ours Chinchilla Eff. Param. Ours 280M 500M 700M 390M 550M 700M 670M 950M 2.2B 12 7 5 8 6 5 10 7 3 25.31 23.91 22. 18.95 18.65 18.48 18.90 19.34 17.73 1.52 1.50 1.45 1.35 1.35 1.30 1.37 1.40 1.34 models with fewer epochs and achieves the best perplexity and downstream performance across all settings. Generalization to external data. To test the generalizability of our scaling law form, we apply it to the published data from Muennighoff et al. (2023), which was completely held out during the development of our scaling law. It therefore represents true test of generalization. They make different choices with respect to backbone architecture (GPT-2 versus Llama-2), tokenization, etc. for pre-training. We find that the additive penalty forms significantly outperforms the Muennighoff et al. (2023) formulations on their own published data, confirming that the improvement generalizes across pre-training implementations.

sections.title: 6 Case study: weight decay improves robustness to data repetition
sections.content: The overfitting coefficient directly quantifies training configurations robustness to data repetition. To demonstrate this, we analyze the effect of weight decay strength on repetition tolerance, training the same grid of model sizes and data budgets at two settings: standard λ = 0.1 and strong λ = 1.0 weight decay, with all other hyperparameters held constant. 7 Preprint. Under review. Figure 6: Strong weight decay incurs single-epoch loss premium. (Left) Computeoptimal frontier: strong weight decay (λ = 1.0) achieves higher loss than the standard setting at every compute budget in the single-epoch regime. (Right) Compute-optimal allocation: strong weight decay favors larger models relative to data. Figure 7: Strong weight decay improves robustness to data repetition. (Left) Fitted overfitting coefficient for standard (λ = 0.1) and strong (λ = 1.0) weight decay; strong weight decay reduces by approximately 70%. (Center, Right) Loss decomposition for 250M-parameter model trained on 100M unique tokens across weight decay values. Single-epoch scaling. We fit separate Chinchilla parameters (A, B, E, α, β) per setting. In the single-epoch regime, strong weight decay incurs loss premium at every compute budget (Figure 6), and its compute-optimal allocation favors larger models relative to data. Scaling under data repetition. We fit our additive penalty (Equation 8) independently for both weight decay settings. Figure 7 (left) shows the fitted values from the one-parameter form (Equation 6): strong weight decay reduces by approximately 70%, meaning it incurs far less overfitting per repetition. The loss decomposition for representative configuration (center, right) shows the overfitting penalty growing superlinearly for both settings, but with significantly lower magnitude under strong weight decay. Crossover under repetition. Although there is single-epoch loss premium, the significantly reduced overfitting cost creates crossover point in performance (Figure 8). At UD = 250M, standard weight decay achieves lower loss at modest compute, but strong weight decay overtakes it at 3.2 1018 FLOPs as the standard settings steeper penalty erodes its single-epoch advantage. Our scaling law predicts this crossover point: the compute budget at which the lower penalty compensates for the single-epoch tax. Prescriptive validation. We validate on held-out configurations  (Table 2)  . The results confirm the crossover dynamics predicted by our law. Near the predicted crossover budget C, the two settings perform comparably. Beyond this point, the gap widens rapidlyat = 1 1019 (3 past the crossover), strong weight decay reduces perplexity by 2.8 points. The same pattern holds at UD = 500M: near the crossover the settings are comparable, but at 8 Preprint. Under review. Figure 8: Strong weight decay outperforms in high-compute regimes. (Left) Computeoptimal allocation frontiers for standard (λ = 0.1) and strong (λ = 1.0) weight decay at UD = 250M. (Right) Compute-optimal loss as function of compute budget. Strong weight decay incurs higher loss at low compute but crosses over at 3.2 1018 FLOPs. Table 2: Weight decay prescriptive validation. For each (token budget, compute budget) pair, we train the configuration recommended by our scaling law (Equation 8). Standard weight decay (λ = 0.1) achieves lower loss at small compute budgets, but strong weight decay (λ = 1.0) enables productive use of additional compute, ultimately achieving lower absolute loss. We denote the expected crossover point under our law with C. UD 250M 31018 500M 11019 3 5 1018 1 1019 1 1019 3 1019 6 6 1.46 1. 23.38 22.93 350M 350M 700M 550M Weight Decay Params Epochs Perplexity BPB λ = 0.1 λ = 1.0 λ = 0.1 λ = 1.0 λ = 0.1 λ = 1.0 λ = 0.1 λ = 1.0 λ = 0.1 λ = 1.0 700M 830M 18.16 16. 23.13 20.34 22.91 21.66 18.52 18.75 5B 2.5B 1.47 1.41 1.52 1. 1.36 1.32 1.35 1.30 3B 1B 2 4 2 6 5 5 4 3 1019 strong weight decay achieves 16.65 vs. 18.16 perplexity. Our law correctly predicts that strong weight decay should train for more epochs, and the recommended configurations achieve lower loss when the data constraint is binding. This provides prescriptive support for the empirical finding of Kim et al. (2026) that optimal weight decay in data-constrained regimes can be an order of magnitude larger than standard practice. Practical implications. The compute-optimal allocation frontier (Figure 5) provides concrete guidance for data-constrained practitioners. Given fixed data budget, there is compute threshold beyond which repeating data further is counterproductive. Past this threshold, compute is better allocated to model capacity. Combined with the finding that strong weight decay reduces the overfitting coefficient by approximately 70%, practitioners in dataconstrained settings have two complementary levers: increasing regularization strength and choosing the right model sizeepoch tradeoff. Limitations. Our study spans model sizes up to 1B parameters and repetition counts up to 16 epochs; we cannot confirm that the fitted exponents hold at the frontier scales used in practice. Our scaling law form may not extrapolate to extreme repetition counts beyond those covered in our study. Notably, our law doesnt encapsulate phenomena such as double descent (Nakkiran et al., 2020). We test only two weight decay values (λ {0.1, 1.0}) and fit independent scaling sweeps to provide prescriptive recommendations. Extending our law to incorporate regularization strength directly is an interesting direction for future work. 9 Preprint. Under review.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: We have presented simple data-constrained scaling law that models the cost of data repetition with simple, additive overfitting penalty. complexity ladder of one-, two-, and four-parameter forms provides Pareto frontier of fit quality versus complexity, with even the simplest form substantially outperforming prior effective-data formulations. The overfitting penalty provides new axis for evaluating training configurations, directly quantifying robustness to data repetition. This property is of increasing importance as compute grows faster than data. As case study, we show that strong weight decay (λ = 1.0) reduces the overfitting penalty by approximately 70%, providing prescriptive guidance that explains, and can replace, the expensive per-configuration hyperparameter sweeps required by prior empirical work (Kim et al., 2026). Finally, our overfitting penalty yields qualitatively new compute-optimal allocation advice: beyond threshold compute budget, further data repetition is counterproductive, and practitioners should scale model capacity instead. Together, these findings provide concrete guidance for the increasingly common setting where high-quality data, not compute, is the binding constraint.

In Proceedings of the 57th annual meeting of the association for computational linguistics, pp. 47914800, 2019. 13 Preprint. Under review. Table 3: Chinchilla baseline quality on Muennighoff et al. (2023) data. Published parameters (fit on Hoffmann et al. (2022)s scraped isoFLOP points) vs. parameters refit on the 29 single-epoch runs from Muennighoff et al. (2023). Metric R2 (all runs) R2 (single-epoch) R2 (multi-epoch) Huber loss Published Refit 0.445 0.711 0.306 0.0331 0.861 0.989 0.795 0.0115 Reanalysis of Muennighoff et al. compute-optimal allocation Our scaling law recommends allocating data-constrained compute toward larger models trained for fewer epochs, while Muennighoff et al. (2023) recommend the opposite: smaller models trained for more epochs. We trace this disagreement to the fact that Muennighoff et al. (2023) fit their Chinchilla base law on Hoffmann et al. (2022)s scraped C4 isoFLOP points rather than on their own scaling runs. Although both studies use C4, differences in tokenization, preprocessing, and training codebases mean that parameters fit on one set of runs need not transfer to another (Li et al., 2025). Indeed, we show that this baseline fits the Muennighoff et al. (2023) single-epoch runs poorly, and that the (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N effective-parameter mechanism primarily compensates for this baseline error. When the Chinchilla base is refit on their own data, the two analyses agree. All results below use the Muennighoff et al. (2023) filtered data split (182 runs) with their outlier-removal criteria, matching their evaluation setup. The Chinchilla baseline transferred from Hoffmann et al. fits poorly. Table 3 compares the published Chinchilla parameters against parameters refit on the 29 single-epoch runs from Muennighoff et al. (2023). The published parameters explain only 71.1% of variance on single-epoch data (R2 = 0.711). Since no repetition is involved, these runs should be well-described by Chinchilla law. Refitting the Chinchilla parameters to the Muennighoff et al. (2023) single-epoch data recovers R2 = 0.989, near-perfect fit. The 3 reduction in Huber loss confirms that the original parameters are poor description of these data. The improvement is not driven by relaxing the α = β constraint: tied refit on the same 29 runs also achieves R2 = 0.989. The effective-parameter mechanism compensates for baseline error. Table 4 shows the impact on the (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N scaling law. With the published Chinchilla base, adding the (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N mechanism improves R2 by +0.346 (from 0.445 to 0.791)appearing to provide substantial value. With the refit base, the same mechanism improves R2 by only +0.070 (from 0.861 to 0.931). The absolute fit quality improves (Huber 0.0158 0.00720), but the marginal contribution of the (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N mechanism is smaller once the baseline is correct. The mechanistic explanation is visible in the fitted parameters. Under the published base, the effective-parameter rate constant = 5.31, meaning effective model size saturates quickly, biasing the optimal allocation toward smaller models. Under the refit base, = 3,294, effectively infinite. The optimizer disables the (cid:98)N mechanism entirely. Without (cid:98)N pulling the allocation toward smaller models, the refit (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N law recommends larger models with fewer epochs which aligns with our independent finding. The allocation recommendation reverses. Figure 9 shows the compute-optimal allocation under the published and refit (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N laws at unique data budget of 25B tokens. The published law (yellow) allocates toward smaller models and more epochs than Chinchilla, consistent with the original recommendation. The refit law (red) reverses direction, allocating toward larger modelsconsistent with our finding that compute is better spent on model capacity than on data repetition. 14 Preprint. Under review. Table 4: Benefit of (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N under published vs. refit Chinchilla base. R2 is the improvement over the corresponding Chinchilla-only baseline. Condition Published Chinchilla + (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N (published) + (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N (refit) Refit Chinchilla + (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N (refit) R2 (all) 0.445 0.772 0.791 0.861 0.931 R2 +0.327 +0. +0.070 R2 (single) R2 (multi) Huber Loss 0.711 0.763 0.789 0.989 0.989 0.306 0.777 0.792 0.795 0. 0.0331 0.0158 0.0158 0.0115 0.00720 Figure 9: Compute-optimal allocation reverses under refit Chinchilla base. Black lines: Chinchilla-optimal (no repetition). Solid lines: (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N-optimal. Yellow: published parameters from Muennighoff et al. (2023). Red: refit parameters. The published (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N law allocates toward smaller models and more data than Chinchilla, while the refit law allocates toward larger models, reversing the original recommendation. UD = 25B tokens. In summary, the apparent disagreement between our recommendation and that of Muennighoff et al. (2023) reduces to the (cid:98)N mechanism absorbing systematic error from misspecified Chinchilla baseline. When the baseline is corrected, the (cid:98)N mechanism disables itself and the allocation recommendation aligns with ours. We note that this reanalysis was possible only because Muennighoff et al. (2023) made their data and experimental details publicly available. 15 Preprint. Under review. Table 5: Model configurations for the scaling study. Total parameters include embeddings Name 15M 25M 35M 50M 125M 250M 500M 750M 1B dmodel 192 256 320 384 768 1,024 1,280 1,536 1,792 Layers Heads 6 8 9 10 12 14 20 24 3 4 5 6 12 16 10 12 14 dhead 64 64 64 64 64 64 128 128 128 dff 768 1,024 1,280 1,536 2,048 2,816 3,584 4,096 4,864 Total Params 15.87M 24.83M 35.30M 48.25M 134.26M 245.60M 488.55M 778.16M 1,128.98M Table 6: Validation-only model configurations, used exclusively for held-out evaluation of the fitted scaling law. Name 280M 350M 390M 720M dmodel 1,024 1,024 1,152 1,408 Layers Heads 17 24 20 25 16 8 9 dhead 64 128 128 128 dff 2,816 2,816 3,072 4,096 Total Params 284.14M 374.07M 392.51M 721.25M

sections.title: B Training details
sections.content: B.1 Architecture All models follow the Llama 2 architecture (Touvron et al., 2023) with multi-head attention (MHA) using Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE), SwiGLU feed-forward networks, and RMSNorm pre-normalization. We use Flash Attention 2 and enable torch.compile for all runs. The vocabulary size is 32,000 tokens (Llama 2 tokenizer). B.2 Model configurations Table 5 lists the nine model sizes used in the scaling study. Table 6 lists four additional configurations used only for held-out validation of the fitted scaling law. B.3 Training hyperparameters Table 7 summarizes the training hyperparameters shared across all runs. The learning rate follows cosine schedule that decays from the peak value to 10% of peak over the full training duration, with linear warmup over the first 1% of steps. B.4 Dataset We train on FineWeb sample-10BT (Penedo et al., 2024), 10 billion token sample of the FineWeb corpus. Documents are packed into fixed-length chunks of 512 tokens with end-ofsequence token separators. B.5 Evaluation We report the final validation loss computed over 6400 validation documents; no early stopping is applied. B.6 Infrastructure All experiments are run on NVIDIA B200 GPUs or NVIDIA A6000 GPUs, with one GPU per run. 16 Preprint. Under review. Table 7: Training hyperparameters. Parameter Value Optimizer Learning rate β1, β2 Weight decay (standard) Weight decay (strong) Gradient clipping LR schedule Min LR ratio Warmup ratio Sequence length Batch size Mixed precision AdamW 2 104 0.9, 0.95 0.1 1.0 1.0 (max norm) Cosine with linear warmup 0.1 0.01 512 128 bf16 Table 8: Single-epoch grid, standard weight decay (λ = 0.1). indicates completed run. 9 model sizes 8 data budgets. Model 50M 100M 200M 400M 800M 1.5B 6B 3B 15M 25M 35M 50M 125M 250M 500M 750M 1B

sections.title: C Experimental grid
sections.content: This appendix details the full experimental grid for both the standard (λ = 0.1) and strong weight decay (λ = 1.0) studies. All runs use the Llama 2 architecture trained on FineWeb with causal language modeling (CLM) objective. The grid spans nine model sizes (15M1B parameters) and eight unique data budgets (50M6B tokens), with multi-epoch runs at up to 16 epochs (RD {0, 1, 3, 7, 11, 15}). C.1 Standard study (λ = 0.1) Phase 1: single-epoch runs. Table 8 shows the single-epoch grid. Larger models are restricted to smaller budgets due to compute constraints, yielding banded design. Phase 2: multi-epoch runs. Table 9 lists the multi-epoch cells. Epoch counts correspond to repetition levels RD = 0, 1, 3, 7, 11, 15. C.2 Strong weight decay study (λ = 1.0) Phase 1: single-epoch runs. Table 10 shows the single-epoch grid for the strong weight decay study. Larger models (750M, 1B) and larger budgets for mid-sized models provide only single-epoch runs, anchoring the Chinchilla baseline without multi-epoch extensions. Phase 2: multi-epoch runs. Table 11 lists the multi-epoch cells for the strong weight decay study. 17 Preprint. Under review. Table 9: Multi-epoch grid, standard weight decay (λ = 0.1). Each row is (model, UD) cell; columns indicate available epoch counts. 1 12 50M 50M 50M 2 4 Model UD 16 50M 25M 100M 25M 200M 25M 50M 100M 200M 125M 100M 125M 200M 125M 400M 125M 800M 250M 100M 250M 200M 250M 400M 250M 800M 500M 100M 500M 200M 500M 400M Table 10: Single-epoch grid, strong weight decay (λ = 1.0). 7 model sizes up to 8 data budgets. Model 25M 50M 125M 250M 500M 750M 1B 50M 100M 200M 400M 800M 1.5B 6B 3B

sections.title: D Scaling law fit details
sections.content: This appendix reports the full numerical results for all scaling law fits discussed in the main text, including Phase 1 (Chinchilla baseline) and Phase 2 (repetition penalty) parameter estimates, goodness-of-fit metrics, and external validation on the Muennighoff et al. (2023) C4 dataset. D.1 Fitting methodology All scaling laws are fit by minimizing Huber loss in log-space with δHuber = 103, using L-BFGS with strong Wolfe line search conditions. To mitigate sensitivity to initialization, we employ grid search: 324 initializations for the Chinchilla base law (a 4 3 3 3 3 grid over E, A, α, B, β) and 7160 initializations for the repetition penalty forms depending on the number of free parameters. We report three variants of the coefficient of determination: R2: standard 1 SSres/SStot, computed over all data points. R2 multi: R2 restricted to multi-epoch runs only (epochs > 1), isolating the models ability to capture repetition effects. single: R2 restricted to single-epoch runs (RD = 0). R2 18 Preprint. Under review. Table 11: Multi-epoch grid, strong weight decay (λ = 1.0). 2 12 1 8 50M 50M 50M Model UD 25M 25M 25M 16 50M 100M 200M 50M 100M 200M 125M 100M 125M 200M 125M 400M 125M 800M 250M 100M 250M 200M 250M 400M 250M 800M 500M 100M 500M 200M 500M 400M Table 12: Phase 1 Chinchilla parameters for the standard (λ = 0.1) and strong weight decay (λ = 1.0) studies. Parameter Standard (λ = 0.1, = 60) Strong WD (λ = 1.0, = 45) α β R2 1.8383 216.58 0.2999 4,964.42 0.4274 0. 2.0422 214.64 0.2922 29,370.43 0.5333 0.9955 Repetition is parameterized as RD = epochs 1 with UD denoting the unique token budget. Phase 1 parameters (E, A, α, B, β) are locked during Phase 2 fitting; only the repetition penalty parameters are optimized. All fits use total parameter counts (embedding + non-embedding). Uncertainties are reported as median absolute deviation (MAD) across bootstrap resamples. D.2 Phase 1: Chinchilla parameters Table 12 reports the Chinchilla baseline parameters for both weight decay conditions. The base law takes the form L(N, D) = + A/Nα + B/Dβ, fit to single-epoch runs only. Strong weight decay (λ = 1.0) raises the entropy floor by 0.2 nats and increases by 6 with steeper data exponent β, consistent with stronger regularization reducing effective model capacity at fixed parameter count. D.3 Phase 2: Repetition penalty parameters Phase 2 fits the repetition penalty on top of the locked Phase 1 parameters. We compare five functional forms: two baselines from prior work and three additive penalty forms from our complexity ladder. Baseline forms. The exponential decay form from Muennighoff et al. (2023) defines effective data (cid:98)D = UD (1 + (1 exp(RD/R))) and evaluates L(N, (cid:98)D) = + A/Nα + B/ (cid:98)Dβ. 19 Preprint. Under review. Penalty (4p) Parameters (value MAD) = 12.731 1.631 = 13.749 1.010, R = 0.00681 0.00069 = 0.00569 0.00042, κ = 1.350 0.106 = 0.00257 1.45 108, δ = 1.563 1.4 105, κ = 1.391 5.5 106, γ = 1.024 1.5 104 = 1,706,066 871 0.9741 0.9737 0.9921 0. 0.9476 0.9466 0.9875 0.9936 0.001463 0.001461 0.000925 0.000860 0.9958 0. 0.000759 The effective parameter extension adds (cid:98)N = UN (1 + ates L( (cid:98)N, (cid:98)D). (1 exp(RN/R N))) and evaluAdditive Penalty forms. The one-parameter form (Equation 6) adds penalty RD (N/UD). The two-parameter form (Equation 7) generalizes to RD (N/UD)κ. The four-parameter form (Equation 8) further generalizes to Rδ (N/Uγ D)κ. Standard study (λ = 0.1). Table 13 reports fits on our FineWeb data (n = 157 total, nmulti = 81 multi-epoch runs). Even the one-parameter additive penalty substantially outperforms both baselines, achieving R2 multi = 0.9503 versus 0.5825 for the exponential decay form. The four-parameter additive penalty reaches R2 = 0.9971. Strong weight decay study (λ = 1.0). Table 14 reports the analogous fits (n = 126 total, nmulti = 81). Two observations stand out. First, the exponential decay baseline performs considerably better under strong WD (R2 multi = 0.9476) than under standard WD (0.5825), reflecting the more benign repetition dynamics when regularization is strong. Second, the four-parameter additive penalty recovers γ 1.02 for strong WD versus 0.64 for standard, indicating that the data-scaling exponent in the penalty term is itself modulated by regularization strength. The exponential decay forms = 12.73 under strong WD versus 7.76 under standard WD confirms that repetition is approximately 60% more tolerable with strong regularization. D.4 External validation on Muennighoff data To verify that our penalty forms generalize beyond our experimental setup, we refit all models on the C4 deduplicated dataset from (Muennighoff et al., 2023) (n = 158, up to 64 epochs). Phase 1 Chinchilla parameters on this data: = 1.9031, = 432.63, α = 0.3362, = 5360.24, β = 0.3868. Table 15 reports Phase 2 results. We additionally report R2 single, the coefficient of determination on single-epoch runs, to confirm that the repetition penalty does not degrade the Chinchilla baseline fit. All forms except (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N achieve identical R2 single = 0.9763 by 20 Preprint. Under review. Table 15: Repetition penalty fits on the Muennighoff et al. (2023) C4 deduplicated data (n = 158, up to 64 epochs). Best values per metric in bold. Form Key Params Exp. Decay ( (cid:98)D) Eff. Param. ( (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N) Add. Penalty (1p) Add. Penalty (2p) Add. Penalty (4p) = 288.1 = 23.82 = 38.71, R = 0.002857 = 0.006670, κ = 0.582 = 2.48 106, δ = 1.040, κ = 0.803, γ = 0.526 R2 0.8953 0. 0.9557 0.9633 R2 multi 0.8442 0.8670 0.9426 0.9549 single 0.9763 0.9832 0.9763 0.9763 Huber 0.008239 0.007987 0.005910 0. 0.9675 0.9617 0.9763 0.004256 construction, since the additive penalty terms vanish at RD = 0. The (cid:98)D, (cid:98)N form attains R2 single = 0.9832 because its (cid:98)N saturation modifies predictions for over-parameterized single-epoch runs. On multi-epoch runs, all three additive penalty forms outperform both baselines, with the four-parameter form achieving the best overall fit (R2 multi = 0.9617). D.5 FLOPs approximation For compute-optimal allocation analysis, we approximate training FLOPs as Ctrain 6 Dtotal = 6 UD (1 + RD). (9) Given fixed compute budget Ctrain and unique data budget UD, we sweep over candidate epoch counts, compute the implied model size = Ctrain/(6 UD epochs), evaluate the predicted loss under each scaling law, and select the allocation that minimizes loss.

sections.title: E Downstream evaluation details
sections.content: This appendix provides evaluation methodology and task-level results supporting the aggregate OLMES Avg. BPB scores reported in Table 1 and Table 2. E.1 Evaluation setup All downstream evaluations use the Open Language Model Evaluation System (OLMES). We evaluate the final checkpoints under the following protocol: Format: 5-shot, Reading Comprehension (RC) Metric: Bits-per-byte (BPB) Max sequence length: 512 tokens We report BPB rather than accuracy because BPB is continuous metric that remains informative even when models perform near chance on multiple-choice tasks, as is common at the model scales in our study (Heineman et al., 2025). E.2 Task inventory Table 16 lists the 19 tasks used to compute the OLMES Avg. BPB. E.3 Task-level BPB breakdown at UD = 250M Table 17 reports per-task BPB for the three matched-compute configurations from the UD = 250M, = 5 1018 comparison in Table 1. E.4 Per-task BPB at UD = 500M Table 18 and Table 19 report per-task BPB for the UD = 500M configurations from Table 1. Preprint. Under review. Table 16: Downstream evaluation tasks.
